title: Revert #3745 and fix missing exit code for journald
body:
#3745 is causing a massive performance regression on systems using the journald events backend. Revert it - the bug isn't worth the perf hit.

Also, fix a bug where we were not properly storing container exit code for container events when using the journald backend - so it would always be returned as 0.

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
Fixes #3795
LGTM

Seems to fix the exit-status behavior, but now the commands in question take 3.5-4.5 seconds to complete???
```
# time podman run --rm alpine /bin/false
real    0m4.100s
user    0m5.327s
sys     0m0.223s
```
Similarly for `podman run` plus separate `podman rm --force`: the `podman rm` exits, then 3 seconds later the `podman run` command exits (with correct exit status).
Eeek. That's the exact opposite of what I was seeing over here...
@edsantiago Is this a short-lived VM? Or a longer-lived system? Trying to figure out what's going on here

mcs@bellerophon code/libpod (test_events_revert) » time sudo ./bin/podman run -t -i --rm fedora ls /doesnotexist                                                                                                                                                                      
ls: cannot access '/doesnotexist': No such file or directory
sudo ./bin/podman run -t -i --rm fedora ls /doesnotexist  0.14s user 0.21s system 22% cpu 1.558 total

Nevermind, I can reproduce locally...
Actually, nevermind - I'm testing against 1.4.4, and the timing seems spot-on identical...
Code Changes LGTM if the timing issues can be figured out.
Hmmmm, good question. The 4-second lag I'm seeing is on my laptop, with 41-day uptime. I have not tried on the virt I created this morning.
@edsantiago Further question - does `podman events --since 1h --until 1s` take a long time to complete (about the same time as `podman run`)?

@mheon indeed it does.

I'm copying my podman binary to the VM I created this morning; looks like another 6-8 minutes left. I will report back on how that runs.
That's it. On fresh VM, all those commands are relatively instantaneous.

Alright, probably length of journal, then.

Maybe we need to add an additional criteria to `GetLastEvent` - check only the last hour of entries, or similar...

@edsantiago pushed a new version that uses only events from the last 15 minutes - hopefully journald's logic will know this means it only needs a very small section of the tail of the logs, and things will be faster.

We can probably reduce that even further - I find it very dubious that it'd take more than a minute for us to successfully grab the event from the journal, for example.
/hold

@mheon I'll try it but FWIW I think it's sizeof(journal), not sizeof(what you ask for), that makes the difference:
```
# for unit in s m h;do /bin/time -f %E podman events --since 10${unit} --until 1s >/dev/null;done
0:03.28
0:03.20
0:03.10
```
i.e. --since 10s, 10m, 10h are the same. I tried 10d, though, and had to ^C after 6 minutes.

dont merge this until we have a chance for discussion ... i have not seen this before and i was using  a journal 3 months old.  also, it won't likely be the the age of the journal,  but much more like likely the number of podman events in the journal.

i'd also like to better understand the problem ... given I wrote the code, I might have some ideas on how to speed things up ... given a problem.  That said, the use of anything like this for events is going to catch up with us.

Yeah, don't merge yet, FWIW I'm trying the new code (with since=15m) and am seeing a hang:
```
# time ..../podman run --rm alpine /bin/false
[several minutes gone by, no sign of life]
```
it would be interesting to understand a reproducer. are we complaining about finding the last event or podman events?
Removed all perf-related commits. Now only contains accuracy fixes for journald exit codes.
/lgtm
@mheon feel free to take the hold off when you are ready!

I ^C'ed the previous version (2nd iteration) after 149 minutes. 

The new version, I'm now seeing consistent 21 seconds for a simple `run -rm`. Instantaneous for just `run`. I'm wondering if the 21 seconds has to do with my letting the old version run so long.

Anyhow, exit status is fixed in both `run -rm` and `podman rm -f RUNNING-CONTAINER`, so LGTM. The lag will have to be addressed later.
/hold cancel
